claims.claim: Run 2.0 data changes deck quality only when source cards become concrete layout, proof, rhythm, and gate decisions.
claims.business_relevance: A public demo must show that the system improves commercial communication, not merely that it collected more references.
qa_checks: Each generated slide references at least one evidence, aesthetic, or asset memory rule.
The comparison slide names the weaker and stronger states and highlights one delta.
No slide claims post-training, fine-tuning, or model learning.

claims.claim: Aesthetic memory should control slide rhythm by varying density, scale, and visual role across cover, setup, contrast, proof, climax, and close.
claims.business_relevance: Commercial buyers judge taste through pacing as much as content; six equal technical panels would make the demo feel like a report.
qa_checks: Narrative spine roles appear in the required six-slide order.
Adjacent slides do not use identical panel counts and scale.
The climax receives visibly larger outcome weight than setup and proof.

claims.claim: Asset memory should use generated images or SVGs only for atmosphere, marks, or diagrams while keeping core text and claims editable in PowerPoint.
claims.business_relevance: The public demo must look premium without sacrificing buyer edits, accessibility review, or release governance.
qa_checks: Core claims, labels, and gate text remain native editable PPT text.
Generated backgrounds contain no essential text.
SVG or diagram assets have provenance and render-risk notes.

claims.claim: A bad aesthetic-memory arm is a useful negative control when it intentionally violates rhythm, density, and hierarchy rules.
claims.business_relevance: The experiment can show that design memory quality matters, not just that extra files or longer prompts improve outputs.
qa_checks: Negative-control brief names the violated memory behavior.
Evaluation separates content completeness from aesthetic control.
The negative control is not presented as a public-ready deck.

claims.claim: Appendix absorption preserves credibility by routing proof detail to notes or appendix material instead of compressing it into smaller main-slide text.
claims.business_relevance: A six-slide public demo needs evidence depth available for review without making the main story feel dense or defensive.
qa_checks: Main slides stay within their density budgets.
Detailed source or QA notes are routed outside the main visible body.
No main slide uses tiny text to compensate for excess proof.

claims.claim: Public release requires both native render verification and a human approval gate before any generated deck is treated as publishable.
claims.business_relevance: The demo is commercial-facing; release governance protects against broken renders, inaccessible assets, and unsupported claims.
qa_checks: Delivery status remains blocked until render checks are recorded.
Human approval is required for public publishing.
Generated artifacts are not committed as final release evidence without the gate.
